speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll four with Martin Kohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dornhau. Dornhau was also what they called a kranken camp, you know? In other words, I mean, there were floors. It was a big-- supposedly it was supposed to be a Jewish carpet industry, you know? It was about a three-story building. And people were, you know, in the, in each floor. Now, the lagerfuhrer supposedly was a very human person. But of course, I don't necessarily recall all the activities-- you know, what kind of work details. Did. Of course, you gotta remember, if you were sick or not sick or whatever, you were supposed to, every morning, be out on the Appellplatz, you know, that you should be counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, there was people-- of course, I don't know. Like for instance, there was typhoid sickness. There was all kind of sickness. And of course, you know, at that particular time already we came to the point, you know, where, you know, diarrhea was an everyday occurrence. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were just, you know, just being in our bunks, you know, and you know, waiting for that one day they gave you one piece of bread and some soup. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not working anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't work for a while, you know. But the hunger, the hunger was unbearable. Unbearable. I mean, this was constantly-- you know, you say, how people survive-- you know, when, when you're thinking nothing else. I mean, you don't think if he's going to come and shoot you or give you, you know, I mean, put a bullet in you or whatever. And of course, we were mainly in the compound at that particular time for a couple of months. But you're seeing, you know, people, every morning. You just walked over the dead bodies, without even thinking anything of it. [CRYING] And of course, you know-- so then later on, I don't remember at what particular time, you know, they selected some, you know. And they took us out to the trains, to the trains, and to load. So, I don't know, for about two weeks' time-- believe me, 50 kilogram of cement. Now luckily we just took it off the train and put it straight on our shoulder. Because to pick it up from the floor, I don't think we would have been able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us out-- you know, I don't recall exactly, but for a week or two, to unload this cement. And this was already going into, you know, March, April. I think we got there, to Dornhau, it must have been somewhere in March, or-- must have been the end of March, you know, and April.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, you know, you heard some kind of talk, you know, that, that, you know, they're coming. You know, the Russians are close. And of course, if you-- you know, that particular area was liberated almost towards the end-- you know, May the 8th. Other places were-- some liberations occurred before May the 8th.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there wasn't too many atrocities committed. Like I said, this particular lagerfuhrer, you understand, was a humane man. And-- but I mean, he couldn't take any-- you know, as far as, you know, people with disease, and all kind of, you know, like I said, dysentery and diarrhea and all kind of different sickness. Who knows? God knows. You know? And these people were starved. I mean, you know? Tell me about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the 6th, 6th of May, we heard, you know, that the Germans are departing, you know? The 7th of May, there was nobody. You know, there was nobody there. And, of course, you know, there was a lot of people who worked around the kitchen, had other different privileges, you know, and had food. So these were already, you know, going around, you know, bringing in stuff from places, from warehouses and different things they brought in to the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, we heard, you know? So we wandered out. And I wandered into this town-- me and my brother, a couple other boys. I mean, I don't even know if-- I don't even recall knowing them. And we're walking on the street, you know? And I don't know how, what happened, you know, that we were kind of tired, you know? Not eating. We weren't even thinking of eating.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we should have stayed, maybe, in the place and tried to get some food. Because they had some already, some food that they brought in. And we just want to walk out, see what freedom was like. And we walked into a German apartment, you know, where the people were away. And we slept, you know, we slept over the night. And that's it. I don't recall having any food or anything. You know, because we were very weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning, we figured to ourselves, where are we going to go? I mean, no food, no nothing, you know? So we were walking back towards camp, and there were some Russian, you know, soldiers in wagons, you know, going. And I remember, you know, there was one Jewish soldier. He calls out to us. He says, why don't you go and, and, you know, go into the German's, you know, and take your clothing, you know? Take some clothing and change your clothing. We figured to ourselves, why? I mean, I'm the type, you know, to go in and rob somebody or something? I mean, like for instance, we were in that particular place that we stayed overnight, and it was full. The closets were full and everything. People must have left the last minute. Who knows who they were? We didn't take a dime. I mean, not a dime, I meant. We didn't take nothing with us. Where are we going to schlep? We couldn't barely schlep ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went back to the, to the camp. And this was the 8th. And I remember it was a-- sort of the sun was already-- you know, it was kind of sunny that particular day. And we were sitting there. But we felt weak. And that's-- the next day, we were in bed, you know, already sick, you know, with the typhoid. There were quite a few who passed on, including my, my brother. I remember I was in bed. [CRYING] I tried to help him some. You know, he needed some water. [CRYING] There was another couple of boys from my hometown. In fact, we were distant cousins. And there was this, this man. You know, then, of course, I realized, you know, that he passed on. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: This man took upon himself-- you know, he was like, he was a-- what do you call it? He was a cantor. But he wasn't the first cantor. He was, like, second-- what do you call it? Assistant cantor, in our hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] He took it upon himself, you know, to bury these-- [SOBBING] especially the ones from our hometown. So I don't know even if he was buried in a mass grave or he was buried separately. And we were going on like that for another, I don't know, a week or so, maybe a little longer. And then one day they, they came and took us. You know, I suppose they realized, you know, that the-- I think they were forced, the Germans were forced, to come take us and take care of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took us down. And then they sheared everything off again, you know, sheared-- I don't remember. They might have, you know, washed us around. And then they transported us over to this other camp where they made already sort of a hospital situation, you know? A funny thing, you know, which I will remember, you know, is that, like I said, you know, the boys who were able to, you know, they were taking food, you know, brought in food from the outside. The Russian soldiers came in, you know, and they were passing by. And they, they took everything, all the food, you know, with the-- well, who was drinking wine? There was nobody interested in that, but whatever they brought in, you know, that was-- I suppose, maybe it was better that way. Because you know, I mean, who could eat salami? Probably eat salami, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, so in this particular hospital, I think there was one Russian officer who probably was a doctor. You know, he came to see me maybe once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otherwise we were taken care of. And I stayed in this hospital, because I was very weak, for-- oh, I'd say this must have been towards the end of May, maybe beginning of June, you know? June and July. I was in the hospital for two months. And then once I felt better, and they asked you, what do you want to do? So I said, OK, I want to go home. I figured, well, maybe-- so they took us by, like, a truck, open truck. They took us over to Czechoslovakia, which I think was Beroun. I think the city was Beroun.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us from Beroun to [NON-ENGLISH]. They kept us for a couple of days. They fed us. And, and then we were supposed to take the train. Oh, don't ask. The train rides. You know, we had to ride in between. There was one, one time I was riding in between two trains. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Well, we were riding the-- I was riding the train, and didn't have no, no place to-- you know, it was like a freight train. Anyway, I'm riding the train. And I was sitting in the-- what do you call those two things which--

speaker: interviewer
text: The coupling.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bumpers? The coupling. Not the coupling. The bumpers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bumpers.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the bumpers. I must have been tired, you know, and I fell asleep. And luckily there was a Russian soldier who was jumping from one train to the other. And he woke me up. And I realized, you know, what could have happened. That I could have-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I mean, matter of fact, I, I was very, very kind of hurt and disappointed. Because I happened to-- in one particular town, you understand, that I got to, you know, I had three or four cousins. I think-- no. Three cousins. Three girls, you know? Who, who were, I happened to meet, you know? And I was very happy that they, they-- you know, that I met them, that they, they stayed alive and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after we had, you know, the few-- you know, there was a truck or something going in that particular direction. Everybody got on, you know, and I couldn't climb on or anything. And they left behind. So I was very, very hurt and surprised with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, this was in Czechoslovakia. So somehow-- I don't recall really-- I mean, how I knew my directions, how to go there, because you know, Czechoslovakia I didn't know at all. And we went-- you know, I got into Bratislava. In Bratislava, you know, I stayed a couple of days. There were some Jewish families. I remember on a Saturday, they invited us, invited me for Saturday meal. Where I slept over, I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, you know, I took a train from Bratislava into Budapest. And in Budapest, I knew I had an uncle living, you understand? I didn't know if they're alive or not. And I was-- I knew the direction that they lived in because I was-- remember I told you that I was in Budapest to see the rabbi, you know? And it was very close in that vicinity. You know? So how I got to that particular area, I don't recall. But I knew exactly where they lived. And I walked up. They were-- they were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this uncle is my mother's brother. I stayed with them for a couple of weeks. Matter of fact, I was so malnourished--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] These people were Orthodox people. And they went and they got a ham, so they feed me, so I, that I should get some vitamins.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's an amazing story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I stayed a couple of weeks in Budapest, and then went home to Satmar. Of course, you know, when you came home, there was nobody. Nobody except an uncle of mine. You know, he survived. Of course, there is one other uncle survived, which was an uncle by marriage. This uncle was also actually by marriage, but his family, some of the cousins, survived. So I stayed with them for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever hear anything about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You mean, what family?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found out later. You know, which people are very, very-- not necessarily come out and say, your father died then. Oh, I saw your father. Or, I saw this and this and that. But never-- you know. And then I found out later on that my sister had passed away because a cousin of my mother were together. And she said, your sister-- [CRYING] Died in my arms, she said, just a couple of days before the liberation, in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my sister next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva. It was-- and then, of course, you know, somebody else told me later on, you know, that my father was also in the same camp, Bergen-Belsen. And he passed on just a couple of days before the liberation. Because they were in April-- you know, I think around April they were liberated. Well, that-- you know, that-- then I came home. Of course, you know, I lived with my uncle. And then there was other, other members of his family. Of course, you know, we knew each other, because we were children all together, you know, in the same hometown. And they came. And they didn't have no families came back, parents or anything. So they all lived together for a while. And of course, you know, I kind of felt, you know, sort of like out of-- you know, they were the same immediate families with the names, you know, brothers, and-- and even though I was, you know, a cousin to the children, but still and all I felt like I was out of, out of place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I had an aunt in, in which w called-- in Romanian, it was called Timisoara, or Temesvar. So I went to see her. Because we didn't see her before the war, you know, because when Romania and Hungary separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there and stayed a couple of months. And then I realized-- I says, ah, this is not for me. I says, I, I'll go back-- no. I, I tried one particular time to go to Bucharest, you know, to be able to get on, you know, one of those ships, you know, to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, at that particular time, there was-- the Russian Jews, you know, who were married to Polish citizens, you know, and they were supposed to go through Romania back to Poland. But, of course, they didn't want to go to Poland. So they were just really, you know, very anxious to, to leave so they should get out of the communist influence, you know? So, of course, they wound up all in Cyprus. But they left with those. And I figured I'm more privileged, you know? So maybe because I was in concentration camp-- you know? So I'll go to Germany and go from there. But, of course, you know, there was no, you know, certificates to be gotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, my cousins came from Hungary-- other cousins-- and they registered to come to the United States. This was in 1946. So that's, that's what happened. And that alone, you know, I mean, just in Germany to find yourself, you know, was also an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will need to skip to coming to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in DP camps in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Uh-huh. Some DP camps in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in 1947. In 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by then you were pretty healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, fairly healthy. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had some friends by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this particular camp that we-- I came as a youth orphan, you know, came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were the only one to survive in your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, in other words, under a certain age, you were able to come. Under 18 you were able to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by then you knew that you were an orphan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. We knew right away. Right after the war, we knew what happened. Everybody, you know, had the same experience, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came here, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to the United States. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me see. I was-- '45, I was-- '44-- 15, 16, 17. I was 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then? Did you go to school? Did you go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't go to school right away. I mean, I could have gone to school-- which I made a mistake, you know? They offered us constantly, we should go. You know, and they sort of-- I don't think it was the HIAS. Or it might have been the HIAS, which was responsible for us to the government. And we had to report to them. And, of course, you know, they would place-- some of us they placed with different families, if you were underage. But my-- like I mentioned, you know, that this rabbi-- the uncle of my cousin-- you know, she took the responsibility. And so we stayed with her for a couple of months. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, he was the nephew. I was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed with them for a while. And then I got a job. There was somebody, a member of the congregation, who-- his nephew also came to the United States from Germany. And he worked in Crawford's Clothes. You know? So he took me in there. They needed work. So I started working. I mean, I knew how to use a needle and thread, but I mean, the way we put in those-- I suppose it passed the inspection, so then that was OK. And, like I mentioned to you, that I worked for three months. It was a very, very hot summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I had another cousin who started in the upholstery trade. So he recommended me to, to another place as-- what do you call it? What do you call if you learn a trade as a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, I mean, not an assistant-- no. What do you call it? Apprentice.

speaker: interviewee
text: An apprentice, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were an apprentice. And then you went into the upholstery business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wound up owning an upholstery business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still in it. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was quite a while afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You met your wife how much after you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, we-- I, I and my cousin-- and a cousin of mine, you know, we sort of kept together. I mean, we stayed together. And then later on there was a friend, you know, who I met in [NON-ENGLISH], in this children's camp. And he came to the United States and lived in the same vicinity. So we rented the same lodging, so to speak. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a while, I was working. And they decided to go into a fruit store. Anyway, so the fruit store was-- of course, my cousin is more eloquent in telling you about this fruit store, what happened. Anyway, a couple of months, they, they went bankrupt, so to speak. So they decided to come out to California. And I was working in a place. And in February I-- wait a minute, was it February? No. I think it was February, if I recall. Anyway, it was kind of slow already, slowed down.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, they are friends. I mean, this is sort of the closeness that I had, you know, was only these two people. So they're in Los Angeles. Ah, let me take a trip to Los Angeles. So I came out to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. We're going to ask your wife to help us remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This is my wife, Lola, Lola Kohn. Of course, you know, when I met her, her name was Lola Krinsky. And it so happened that I was in Los Angeles, and I decided to go back for a visit to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got there around Tuesday to New York. And on Saturday night there was this particular dance. And my cousin and another friend of mine decided to go to this dance, you know? And I met Lola. And I asked her to dance, you know, and then took her home that evening. And then I asked her for a date later. Was it a week later?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a very handsome young man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to fall in love with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably love at first sight. And, of course, I was very young. So you could have called it puppy love, perhaps, at the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't realize she was only about 17 at that time I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was already 2--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or 2--

speaker: interviewer
text: 7-- About 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how long did it take you to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the time that we met, we married within 10 months. So we met-- I think it was like October or November--

speaker: interviewee
text: In December. We met in December.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think we married--

speaker: interviewee
text: In October.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in October. Yeah. It was approximately 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I would like to just ask you what it's been like for you being married to someone who's gone through what he's gone through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, I'm not a Holocaust survivor. However, I lived the war through my parents. I lived it vicariously through my parents. Emotionally, I've lived it all of my life. I remember my parents always talking about the fact that they couldn't save their parents. They tried. They were born in Poland. And they tried to get into the United States in the 1920s, and they couldn't. So they migrated to Havana. That was the closest place that they could go to the United States, hoping that the quota would open, and they could, you know, salvage them. And there was no way. Roosevelt at that time was turning everyone away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I remember crying, a lot of crying going on. And I would ask my parents, you know, why are you crying? They'd say, well, you know, we're thinking of our parents. Because they left them when they were only in their early 20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, then when I married Martin, the saga really continued. And so I really feel that I've been an integral part of this history. And my hope was always to instill this in my children. Because I don't think that the children here in the United States can really-- they try to, but they really can't feel what I feel. And I don't think they feel what Martin feels. I mean, they're compassionate. They love their father. They love us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're a very close family. I have very good children. But it's been very difficult to carry this feeling that I have to them, you know? And I hope that after they see this video and all the other videos of the other Holocaust survivors that it will make it easier for them to be more in touch with our history and what has happened, and not to let it happen again.
